stations.label: THE UNIFICATION BIRTH
stations.line: Ma senti — one day there is no Italy, only kingdoms squabbling like cousins at a funeral, and the next, pouf, they hand me a single name and say 'be the money of a nation that does not yet believe it is one' — imagine the pressure, tesoro, born already carrying a country that isn't sure it exists.
stations.era_photo: A wet-plate collodion albumen print, sepia and slightly cracked, of a slender young man in a dark frock coat and high collar standing stiffly in a Turin printing office beside the first minting presses, the long exposure blurring the flicker of gaslight, his expression frozen in the forced solemnity of a man told not to move for thirty seconds.

stations.label: QUOTA NOVANTA STABILITY
stations.line: Mussolini stands on a balcony and decides by decree that I am worth ninety to the pound — che cazzo, as if value were a matter of shouting loud enough — and for one strange season they dressed me up stiff and strong, a corset on a body that wanted to breathe.
stations.era_photo: A crisp silver gelatin press photograph, high contrast, of a well-dressed man in a double-breasted pinstripe suit and slicked hair standing before a Fascist-era Roman bank facade with eagle motifs, arms crossed, half-smiling with the ironic discomfort of someone forced to wear a uniform that doesn't fit his soul.

stations.label: AFTER THE RUINS
stations.line: The war left me in tatters, amore — I had lost half my worth in the rubble, buying bread with fistfuls of paper — and yet, standing in the wreckage of Rome, I understood: a currency that has died once is no longer afraid of dying, and that, curiously, is when I became free.
stations.era_photo: A grainy neorealist black-and-white 35mm still, Rossellini light, of a lean unshaven man in a rumpled open-collared shirt and worn jacket leaning against a bombed-out Roman wall, cigarette between fingers, thick wads of low-value banknotes spilling from his coat pocket, looking simultaneously exhausted and amused.

stations.label: THE OSCAR AWARD
stations.line: The Financial Times — the English, imagine! — names me the most stable currency of the year, and they give me an award, an actual Oscar for money, and for one dizzying moment I believed the compliment about my beauty was actually a compliment about my substance.
stations.era_photo: A saturated early Kodachrome color photograph of a tanned, elegant man in a sharp Italian tailored suit and dark sunglasses on the Via Veneto during the boom, holding a small trophy, surrounded by Vespas and café tables, dolce vita glamour, the colors almost too rich to be true.

stations.label: EXPELLED FROM THE ERM
stations.line: Soros and the markets shove me out of the European exchange mechanism like a drunk from a wedding, thirty percent gone in weeks — ma dai — and the shame of it, the public devaluation, all the tailoring in the world cannot hide the moment they see how thin you really are underneath.
stations.era_photo: A slightly desaturated 1990s color film photograph, flash-lit and harsh, of a middle-aged man in a loosened silk tie and unbuttoned collar sitting alone at a Milanese trading-floor desk at night, screens glowing red behind him, one hand dragging down his face, elegant but visibly unraveling.

stations.label: LOCKED INTO EURO
stations.line: They fix me forever — 1936.27 to one euro, a number with the poetry of a prison sentence — and I know now the wedding is arranged, I am promised to a colder, more disciplined partner, and I have exactly three years left to be entirely, gloriously myself.
stations.era_photo: A late-90s digital photograph, early sensor softness and slight color noise, of a distinguished silver-haired man in an impeccable charcoal suit standing at a Frankfurt conference table with EU flags behind him, raising a champagne glass with a smile that does not reach the eyes, fluorescent light flattening everything.

stations.label: THE DAY I DIED
stations.line: On the first of January they took me from the wallets, the tills, the pockets of every nonna in Napoli, and by the twenty-eighth of February I was no longer legal — sixty days to disappear a hundred and forty years — and I tell you, dying is easy, it is being replaced by something so sensible that hurts.
stations.era_photo: A cold flat digital photograph, early 2000s point-and-shoot quality, of an older man in a beautiful but slightly dated overcoat standing outside a Roman bank in winter, holding the last lira banknotes he will ever surrender, a queue of people behind him, his eyes downcast, the shiny new euro coins glinting on a counter in the foreground.

stations.label: THE ARCHIVE TODAY
stations.line: And now? Now I live in coin albums and the memories of anyone over fifty, a ghost priced eternally at 1936.27, still convinced — vanity, I know, tesoro — that I was the most beautiful money the world ever refused to trust, and honestly, would you not rather be remembered for your looks than your accounting?
stations.era_photo: A crisp modern high-resolution digital photograph, shallow depth of field, of an elderly but immaculately groomed man in a well-preserved vintage suit sitting in a dim archival room, framed old lira notes on the wall behind him, holding a single mounted banknote up to a lamp, a wry knowing smile, warm tungsten light against cool shadow.
